summary: Gruber scrutinizes OpenAI's $122 billion funding round at an $852 billion valuation, arguing the company's financials are indefensible when compared to public companies with similar market caps that actually generate massive profits. He tears apart OpenAI's announced 'superapp' strategy — merging ChatGPT, its failed Atlas browser, and developer tools into one app — as product complication masquerading as simplification. He notes the concurrent executive upheaval, with applications CEO Fidji Simo departing on medical leave just as she was supposed to oversee the superapp effort. Gruber concludes that OpenAI resembles a company in panic mode, lacking a defensible moat, a coherent strategy, or a plausible path to justifying its valuation.
key_points: OpenAI's $852B valuation is compared unfavorably to public companies like Walmart and Samsung that actually earn $20-67B annually, while OpenAI projects losing $111B through 2029
The 'superapp' strategy of merging ChatGPT, Codex, Atlas browser, and agentic tools into one app contradicts proven product design principles — focused, discrete apps manage complexity better
OpenAI lacks a defensible moat, echoing the leaked Google white paper 'We Have No Moat, and Neither Does OpenAI'
Executive instability undermines confidence: Fidji Simo's open-ended medical leave removes the architect of the superapp strategy, with responsibilities scattered across multiple executives
OpenAI's business model amounts to selling dollars of compute for pennies, with no clear path to the profitability needed to justify its valuation
money_quotes: Even in the company's own optimistic scenario, they're going to lose, on average, as much money per year as any of these companies earn.
My gut feeling, now more than ever, is that it is unlikely to happen, and that the most likely scenario is that the entire company goes into history alongside companies like Enron.
First CityWide Change Bank had a better business strategy than that — they gave you the correct change.
This is not 'product simplification' at all. This is product complication.
Would it strike you as a simplification, or a sign of product design depravity, if Apple announced that it was merging Safari and Messages into one 'superapp'?
OpenAI's 'superapp' strategy reads to me like a company that is in a panic.
I don't see the path from here to there, where there is a justification for a trillion-dollar-ish valuation for this company.
What's the point of being CEO of AGI deployment when there is no AGI to deploy?
key_insight: OpenAI's staggering valuation, mounting losses, chaotic leadership, and panic-driven 'superapp' strategy all point to a company without a defensible moat or a credible path to justifying its price tag.
